title: Model Compression
description: Quantization (PTQ, QAT), pruning (structured, unstructured), knowledge distillation, weight sharing, ONNX, and deployment optimization for efficient inference.
details: Model Compression reduces the size and inference cost of deep neural networks while preserving accuracy — critical for deploying models on mobile, edge, and latency-sensitive systems. This file covers quantization (post-training quantization, quantization-aware training, uniform vs non-uniform, symmetric vs asymmetric, INT8 vs FP8, GPTQ, AWQ), pruning (unstructured magnitude pruning, structured channel/layer pruning, lottery ticket hypothesis, iterative pruning, dynamic sparsity), knowledge distillation (teacher-student training, logit matching, hint layers, self-distillation, online distillation), weight sharing and low-rank factorization (SVD for linear layers, CP decomposition, tensor-train), ONNX and model conversion, hardware-aware optimization (NVIDIA TensorRT, Apple CoreML, Google TFLite, Qualcomm SNPE), and evaluation of size-speed-accuracy tradeoffs. Model Compression is increasingly asked in ML engineering and production ML interviews.

sections.title: Quantization: FP32 to INT8
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: Neural networks are surprisingly tolerant to reduced numerical precision. Instead of 32-bit floats, use 8-bit integers for activations and weights. This 4x memory reduction also speeds up inference on hardware with INT8 support (most modern GPUs, CPUs, mobile chips).

Quantization math:

  • Map real values r to quantized q: r = S (q - Z). S = scale factor (float), Z = zero point (integer, usually 0 or 128 for symmetric).
  • Symmetric: Z = 0. Range [-127, 127] for INT8. Simple, used for weights.
  • Asymmetric: Z can be non-zero. Range fits the exact min/max of the tensor. Better for activations (ReLU outputs are all positive, so asymmetric captures the full range).
  • Per-tensor: One scale for the entire weight tensor. Simple, but loses accuracy for outlier channels.
  • Per-channel: One scale per output channel. Better accuracy, more calibration data needed.

Post-Training Quantization (PTQ):

  • Apply after training. Calibrate scales and zero points on a small calibration dataset.
  • Min-max calibration: Set range based on observed min/max. Simple but outlier-sensitive.
  • Percentile calibration: Clip top and bottom 0.1% of values. Reduces outlier impact.
  • KL divergence calibration: Choose quantization thresholds that minimize KL divergence between original and quantized distribution. Used by NVIDIA TensorRT.
  • PTQ typically loses 1–3% accuracy for INT8. For sensitive models (e.g., LLMs), accuracy loss can be larger.

Quantization-Aware Training (QAT):

  • Simulate quantization during training using fake quantization (rounding + clamping) in the forward pass. Backward pass uses straight-through estimator (STE): round() has zero gradient, so treat it as identity.
  • Train with fake quant → export to INT8 with minimal accuracy loss.
  • Essential for aggressive quantization (INT4, binary) or sensitive architectures.
sections.codeBlock:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import numpy as np

# === Quantization functions ===
def quantize(r, scale, zero_point, num_bits=8):
    """Quantize float to integer."""
    q_min = 0
    q_max = 2**num_bits - 1
    q = torch.round(r / scale + zero_point)
    q = torch.clamp(q, q_min, q_max)
    return q.int()

def dequantize(q, scale, zero_point):
    """Dequantize integer to float."""
    return scale * (q.float() - zero_point)

# === Symmetric quantization (for weights) ===
def symmetric_quantize_weights(W, num_bits=8):
    """Per-channel symmetric quantization."""
    q_max = 2**(num_bits - 1) - 1  # 127 for INT8
    
    # Per-channel scale: max absolute value per output channel
    scales = W.abs().max(dim=1, keepdim=True)[0] / q_max
    
    # Quantize
    qW = torch.round(W / scales)
    qW = torch.clamp(qW, -q_max - 1, q_max)
    return qW.int(), scales

# === Fake quantization (QAT) ===
class FakeQuantize(nn.Module):
    """Simulate quantization during training (STE)."""
    def __init__(self, num_bits=8):
        super().__init__()
        self.num_bits = num_bits
        self.q_scale = nn.Parameter(torch.tensor(1.0), requires_grad=True)
        self.q_zero = nn.Parameter(torch.tensor(0.0), requires_grad=False)
    
    def forward(self, x):
        if not self.training:
            return x  # no quantization during inference
        
        q_min = 0
        q_max = 2**self.num_bits - 1
        
        # Quantize
        q = torch.round(x / self.q_scale + self.q_zero)
        q = torch.clamp(q, q_min, q_max)
        
        # Dequantize (backward uses STE: gradient passes through)
        r = self.q_scale * (q - self.q_zero)
        
        # STE: forward = quantized, backward = identity
        return x + (r - x).detach()

# === Quantized Linear layer (QAT-ready) ===
class QuantLinear(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, in_features, out_features, num_bits=8):
        super().__init__()
        self.weight = nn.Parameter(torch.randn(out_features, in_features))
        self.fake_quant = FakeQuantize(num_bits)
    
    def forward(self, x):
        q_weight = self.fake_quant(self.weight)
        return F.linear(x, q_weight)

# === Post-Training Quantization (simplified) ===
def calibrate_and_quantize(model, calib_loader, num_bits=8):
    """PTQ: calibrate then quantize all Linear/Conv layers."""
    model.eval()
    
    # Collect activation statistics
    activations = {}
    hooks = []
    def hook_fn(name):
        def hook(module, input, output):
            activations[name] = output.detach()
        return hook
    
    for name, module in model.named_modules():
        if isinstance(module, (nn.Linear, nn.Conv2d)):
            hooks.append(module.register_forward_hook(hook_fn(name)))
    
    # Run calibration
    with torch.no_grad():
        for x, _ in calib_loader:
            model(x)
    
    # Compute quantization ranges per layer
    ranges = {}
    for name, act in activations.items():
        ranges[name] = {'min': act.min().item(), 'max': act.max().item()}
    
    for hook in hooks:
        hook.remove()
    
    return ranges

# === GPTQ (post-training quantization for LLMs) ===
# Optimal Brain Quantization: minimize quantization error per layer
# using Hessian-based weight update (H = 2 * X^T X for MSE)
# Key steps per layer:
# 1. Compute Hessian H = 2 * X^T X of layer inputs
# 2. For each column of weights w:
#    - Quantize w_j -> w_q_j
#    - Update remaining weights: delta_w = -(w_j - w_q_j) / H_jj * H[:, j]
# This compensates for quantization error in remaining weights
# 
# from auto_gptq import AutoGPTQForCausalLM
# model = AutoGPTQForCausalLM.from_pretrained('model', quantize_config)

sections.title: Pruning: Magnitude, Structured & Lottery Ticket
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: Most neural network weights are redundant — you can remove them without significant accuracy loss. Pruning identifies and removes these redundant weights, creating sparse models that are smaller and (with hardware support) faster.

Unstructured pruning:

  • Remove individual weights based on their magnitude: keep top-k% by absolute value. Can reach 90–95% sparsity with minimal accuracy loss (for large models).
  • Problem: Creates irregular sparsity. No speedup on standard hardware (GPUs love dense matrices). Only benefits with specialized sparse hardware (NVIDIA's 2:4 sparsity, Intel's AMX).

Structured pruning:

  • Remove entire channels, filters, or layers. Produces dense smaller tensors. Direct speedup on any hardware.
  • Channel pruning: Score each channel by importance (L1 norm of weights, BN scaling factor γ, gradient-based importance). Remove low-importance channels.
  • Layer pruning: Remove entire layers (attention heads, FFN layers). For Transformers, can remove middle layers with minimal loss.
  • Block/pillar pruning: Remove contiguous blocks. Useful for N:M sparsity (NVIDIA's 2:4 pattern: 2 non-zeros in every 4 elements).

Lottery Ticket Hypothesis (LTH):

  • Dense networks contain sparse “winning ticket” subnetworks that, when trained in isolation, can match the original network's accuracy.
  • Finding tickets: Train network → prune lowest-magnitude weights → reset remaining weights to their initial values → train again. Repeat. The winning ticket is the sparse subnetwork discovered.
  • Winning tickets exist for many architectures but benefit from structured pruning at scale. The iterative rewinding version (rewind to early training checkpoint, not initialization) is more practical.
sections.codeBlock:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.utils.prune as prune
import numpy as np

# === Magnitude pruning (unstructured) ===
def magnitude_prune(model, sparsity=0.5):
    """Remove smallest weights by absolute value."""
    for name, module in model.named_modules():
        if isinstance(module, nn.Linear):
            prune.l1_unstructured(module, name='weight', amount=sparsity)
    return model

# === Remove pruning reparameterization (make permanent) ===
def remove_pruning(model):
    for name, module in model.named_modules():
        if isinstance(module, nn.Linear):
            try:
                prune.remove(module, 'weight')
            except ValueError:
                pass

# === Global magnitude pruning (not per-layer) ===
def global_magnitude_prune(model, sparsity=0.5):
    """Prune smallest weights globally across all layers."""
    # Collect all weights
    all_weights = []
    for _, module in model.named_modules():
        if isinstance(module, nn.Linear):
            all_weights.append(module.weight.abs().view(-1))
    
    all_weights = torch.cat(all_weights)
    threshold = torch.quantile(all_weights, sparsity)
    
    # Prune weights below threshold
    for _, module in model.named_modules():
        if isinstance(module, nn.Linear):
            mask = module.weight.abs() > threshold
            module.weight.data *= mask.float()

# === Structured channel pruning (via BN gamma) ===
# Train with L1 regularization on BN scaling factors gamma
# After training: prune channels with small gamma
class PrunableBatchNorm(nn.BatchNorm2d):
    """BN with learnable pruning gate via gamma."""
    def forward(self, x):
        # Standard BN
        output = super().forward(x)
        # L1 penalty on gamma is added externally to loss
        return output

# === Structured pruning by channel importance ===
def channel_importance(model, loader):
    """Estimate importance of each channel via activation variance."""
    model.eval()
    importance = {}
    
    hooks = []
    def hook_fn(name):
        def hook(module, input, output):
            # Channel importance = mean absolute activation across batch
            if len(output.shape) == 4:  # Conv: [B, C, H, W]
                imp = output.abs().mean(dim=(0, 2, 3))
            else:  # Linear: [B, C]
                imp = output.abs().mean(dim=0)
            if name not in importance:
                importance[name] = []
            importance[name].append(imp.detach())
        return hook
    
    for name, module in model.named_modules():
        if isinstance(module, (nn.Conv2d, nn.Linear)):
            hooks.append(module.register_forward_hook(hook_fn(name)))
    
    with torch.no_grad():
        for x, _ in loader:
            model(x)
    
    for hook in hooks:
        hook.remove()
    
    # Average importance across batches
    return {k: torch.stack(v).mean(dim=0) for k, v in importance.items()}

# === Lottery Ticket (simplified iterative) ===
def find_winning_ticket(model, train_fn, sparsity=0.8, rounds=5):
    """Iterative magnitude pruning + rewinding."""
    # Initial training
    train_fn(model)  # train to convergence
    initial_weights = {k: v.clone() for k, v in model.state_dict().items()}
    
    for r in range(rounds):
        # Prune
        global_magnitude_prune(model, sparsity ** ((r + 1) / rounds))
        
        # Rewind to initial weights (masked)
        state = model.state_dict()
        for k in state:
            state[k] = initial_weights[k] * state[k].bool().float()
        model.load_state_dict(state)
        
        # Retrain
        train_fn(model)
    
    return model

# === 2:4 structured sparsity (NVIDIA Ampere) ===
# def apply_2_4_sparsity(tensor):
#     """Keep top-2 weights in every group of 4."""
#     shape = tensor.shape
#     tensor = tensor.view(-1, 4)
#     _, indices = torch.topk(tensor.abs(), k=2, dim=1)
#     mask = torch.zeros_like(tensor)
#     mask.scatter_(1, indices, 1)
#     return (tensor * mask).view(shape)

sections.title: Knowledge Distillation
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: A large, accurate teacher model teaches a compact student model to mimic its outputs. The student learns not just the correct labels (hard targets) but also the relative probabilities between classes (soft targets), which contain rich information (e.g., “this image is slightly more horse-like than donkey-like”).

Standard KD loss: L = α · Lhard(ystudent, ytrue) + (1 - α) · Lsoft(ystudent / T, yteacher / T)

  • T = temperature: Higher T produces softer probability distributions (more information about relative similarities between classes). T=1 is standard softmax. T=2–8 is common for distillation.
  • α controls the tradeoff between hard labels and teacher soft targets.
  • Match logits directly: β · ||zteacher - zstudent||² (MSE between pre-softmax logits).

Variants:

  • Hint learning: Match intermediate layer representations (not just final outputs). Teacher's mid-layer features guide the student's corresponding layer.
  • Self-distillation: The same architecture distills from itself (train → prune → retrain with teacher = original). Often improves accuracy.
  • Online distillation: Two student networks teach each other simultaneously. Both networks are peers, no fixed teacher. DML (Deep Mutual Learning).
  • Distillation for LLMs: Fine-tune a small model to mimic a large model's responses. Used in DistilBERT (40% smaller, 97% performance), TinyBERT, MiniLM.
sections.codeBlock:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F

# === Knowledge Distillation Loss ===
class DistillationLoss(nn.Module):
    """
    L = alpha * CE(student_logits, hard_label)
        + (1-alpha) * KL(student_soft, teacher_soft) * T^2
    """
    def __init__(self, temperature=4.0, alpha=0.1):
        super().__init__()
        self.temperature = temperature
        self.alpha = alpha
    
    def forward(self, student_logits, teacher_logits, labels):
        # Hard label loss (standard CE)
        hard_loss = F.cross_entropy(student_logits, labels)
        
        # Soft target loss (KL divergence)
        student_soft = F.log_softmax(student_logits / self.temperature, dim=1)
        teacher_soft = F.softmax(teacher_logits / self.temperature, dim=1)
        soft_loss = F.kl_div(student_soft, teacher_soft, reduction='batchmean')
        # Multiply by T^2 to scale gradients correctly
        soft_loss = soft_loss * self.temperature ** 2
        
        return self.alpha * hard_loss + (1 - self.alpha) * soft_loss

# === Training with distillation ===
def train_distillation(student, teacher, train_loader, val_loader, epochs=10):
    teacher.eval()  # freeze teacher
    student.train()
    
    optimizer = torch.optim.Adam(student.parameters(), lr=1e-4)
    criterion = DistillationLoss(temperature=4.0, alpha=0.1)
    
    for epoch in range(epochs):
        for x, y in train_loader:
            optimizer.zero_grad()
            
            student_logits = student(x)
            with torch.no_grad():
                teacher_logits = teacher(x)
            
            loss = criterion(student_logits, teacher_logits, y)
            loss.backward()
            optimizer.step()

# === Self-distillation ===
def self_distill(model, train_loader, n_rounds=3):
    """
    Round 1: train model normally.
    Round 2+: use previous round's model as teacher.
    """
    teachers = []
    for r in range(n_rounds):
        if r == 0:
            # Normal training
            train_epochs(model, train_loader)
        else:
            # Distill from previous round
            teacher = teachers[-1]
            train_distillation(model, teacher, train_loader, None)
        teachers.append(copy.deepcopy(model))
    return model

# === Online distillation (DML: Deep Mutual Learning) ===
class DML:
    """Two students train collaboratively."""
    def __init__(self, model1, model2, temperature=4.0):
        self.model1 = model1
        self.model2 = model2
        self.temperature = temperature
    
    def train_step(self, x, y):
        logits1 = self.model1(x)
        logits2 = self.model2(x)
        
        # Hard label loss
        loss1_hard = F.cross_entropy(logits1, y)
        loss2_hard = F.cross_entropy(logits2, y)
        
        # Mutual learning loss (KL between each other's soft outputs)
        soft1 = F.log_softmax(logits1 / self.temperature, dim=1)
        soft2 = F.softmax(logits2 / self.temperature, dim=1)
        loss_mutual = F.kl_div(soft1, soft2, reduction='batchmean')
        # Symmetric
        soft1_ = F.softmax(logits1 / self.temperature, dim=1)
        soft2_ = F.log_softmax(logits2 / self.temperature, dim=1)
        loss_mutual += F.kl_div(soft2_, soft1_, reduction='batchmean')
        loss_mutual *= self.temperature ** 2 / 2
        
        total_loss = loss1_hard + loss2_hard + loss_mutual
        return total_loss
